name: Briggs
description: The Briggs method is a CP-first Roux variant created by Joseph Briggs in 2015. It starts with CPFB (corner permutation + first block), builds a 1x2x2 square on the right side, then inserts a pair while simultaneously orienting corners and creating an arrow on the last layer. The final step is a reduced last layer that benefits from pre-solved corner permutation. Briggs was one of the earlier methods to explore the CP-first concept that has since gained traction in the method development community.

steps.name: CPFB (Corner Permutation + First Block)
steps.description: Solve corner permutation for all corners while simultaneously building a 1x2x3 first block on the left side. This is the most planning-intensive step and determines the efficiency of the rest of the solve.
tips: Plan as much as possible during inspection
Learn to identify the 12 corner permutation cases
Practice combining CP with Roux-style first block building
Color neutrality is especially beneficial here

steps.name: 1x2x2 Square
steps.description: Build a 1x2x2 block (one corner-edge pair plus one edge) on the right side of the cube. This partially fills the second block and sets up the next step.
tips: Use R and U moves to minimize disruption
Plan this step during CPFB execution

steps.name: Pair Insertion + CO + Arrow
steps.description: Insert the last right-block pair while simultaneously orienting the remaining corners and creating an 'arrow' pattern on the last layer (one correctly oriented edge adjacent to the solving slot). This multi-purpose step is the key innovation of the Briggs method.
tips: This step combines multiple goals into one
Recognition requires tracking the pair, corners, and LL edges
Practice the specific algorithms for each case
The arrow pattern simplifies the final step

steps.name: Reduced Last Layer
steps.description: Solve the remaining last layer pieces. With corner permutation and orientation already solved and an arrow established, the last layer is reduced to a small set of edge-only cases, solvable with M and U moves.
tips: Very few cases compared to full LL
M and U moves dominate this step
Similar to LSE in Roux but further reduced
Fast execution due to ergonomic moveset

pros: Reduced last layer due to CP-first approach
Good ergonomics with RU and MU movesets
Pioneering CP-first design that influenced later methods
cons: Pair + CO + Arrow step has many cases
Steep learning curve for CP recognition
creator: Joseph Briggs
